finding: withholding information reduced perceived trustworthiness and increased negative judgment of the concealer, even when concealment was adaptive; the effect was driven by reduced trustworthiness attributions, yet decision-makers did not anticipate this consequence

individuals with high certainty-seeking motives negatively evaluated and allocated fewer resources to people who violated established group stereotypes, but only when stereotypes were cognitively functional; heightened certainty-seeking induced the effect across different baseline orientations
inferences about life-history behavior tracked ecological context rather than racial category when both were provided; ecological information overrode race-based stereotypes in determining judgments
memories of unethical behavior became progressively less clear and vivid over time due to psychological distress; this obfuscation increased the likelihood of repeated unethical behavior

a brief intervention encouraging empathic rather than punitive discipline mindsets halved suspension rates and bolstered respect among high-risk students; the effect operated through changes in teacher mindset

uncalculating decision-making increased perceived trustworthiness and actual trustworthiness when visible to others; the effect was mediated by observer inferences of trustworthiness from the transparency of reasoning

allocators overestimated the impact of procedural fairness on recipients' well-being; recipients preferred larger unfair allocations to smaller fair ones, but allocators could not shift to this post-allocation perspective

subjective experience of low socioeconomic status increased appetite and caloric intake independent of actual material constraints; the effect reflects adaptation to perceived deprivation for critical social resources

past-focused environmental comparisons reduced the ideological gap in climate-action attitudes and behavior, whereas future-focused comparisons did not; past-focused messaging leveraged conservatives' temporal orientation
tangible thank-you gifts reduced donation rates, particularly among prior high donors; the effect operated through attention-driven shifts away from intrinsic motives toward cost-benefit reasoning

facial appearance of science communicators influenced lay judgments of scientific quality and selection of science content; perceived competence and morality increased both interest and quality judgments, while attractiveness increased interest but decreased quality perception

stronger belief in free will increased the tendency to overestimate internal relative to external factors when attributing others' behavior; the effect predicted prescribed punishment and reward through the correspondence bias
the adaptive balance between flexible and rigid punishment strategies depended on learning dynamics; flexibility enabled differentiation but risked suboptimal play in competitive settings
perceiving victims as less human increased instrumental violence aimed at goal achievement, but not moral violence; morally motivated perpetrators humanized victims to justify righteous harm
exposure to information about rising economic inequality shifted causal attributions toward structural rather than individual factors, increasing support for policies to redress inequality

temporary-sharing affordances increased disinhibition in self-disclosures by reducing privacy concerns, yet observers' impressions were driven by the revealed content itself, not the sharing mode; disinhibited sharers made worse impressions that persisted after deletion

comparative search strategies (comparing features across options) increased patience in intertemporal choice and susceptibility to temporal framing, while integrative search decreased patience; manipulating search strategy causally affected discounting

individuals overestimated progress toward racial economic equality; overestimation was driven by perceived current equality exceeding reality and was reduced by salience of discrimination but increased by anchoring to own circumstances

brief training in cognitive strategies emphasizing negative consequences of unhealthy foods and positive benefits of healthy foods reduced overall food consumption and improved food choice; effects generalized to untrained items

exposure to opposing beliefs in structured bipartisan social networks improved accuracy in interpreting scientific information and reduced polarization, but this improvement was reversed when partisanship was made salient
